# The Archivist's Warning

The founding-era maps told a different story.

Tessa spread the oldest survey across the reading table, its edges brown with age, and traced the boundary lines with one careful finger. The village was there — marked in the precise hand of someone who had stood on that ground and measured it. Three streets, a well, a mill. Population: forty-two.

She pulled the modern survey alongside it. The same coordinates showed nothing. Empty terrain. As if forty-two people and their homes had simply ceased to exist.

"The maps don't lie," she said to the empty archive. But someone had made them stop telling the truth.
